title: The Vampire of Sacramento: Richard Chase 🕯
segments: Between December 29, 1977 and January 27, 1978, a 27-year-old man named Richard Trenton Chase killed six people in Sacramento, California. He drank some of their blood. He believed his own blood was turning to powder. He had been let out of a state mental hospital months earlier. Investigators called him the Vampire of Sacramento.

Chase was born in Santa Clara County in 1950. From early adolescence he showed signs of severe mental illness. He believed his organs were shifting in his body. He believed his pulmonary artery had been stolen. He believed his blood needed replacement to stay alive.

By his early 20s Chase had been institutionalized multiple times. He had captured and killed small animals. He had injected rabbit blood into his veins. His treating psychiatrists at Beverly Manor in Auburn warned of severe risk if he were released.

Despite those warnings, Chase was discharged in 1977. He returned to his mother's care. His mother weaned him off antipsychotic medication on the belief they were harmful. By late 1977 his condition had collapsed.

On December 29, 1977, Chase shot Ambrose Griffin, 51, while Griffin was helping his wife unload groceries from their car in East Sacramento. The shooting appeared random. Sacramento police initially could not connect it to anything.
On January 23, 1978, Chase entered the home of Teresa Wallin, 22, who was three months pregnant. He shot and killed her. He then mutilated her body and consumed some of her blood.

Sacramento Police Department investigators were horrified at the scene. The escalation from a random shooting to an indoor killing with consumption suggested an extreme offender — and one whose pattern would repeat.

FBI Special Agent Robert Ressler, then helping develop the FBI's behavioral analysis program, was called in by Sacramento. Ressler studied the Wallin scene and produced a written profile. He predicted the killer was a young white male in his 20s, mentally ill, lived alone, drove a junk car, and had likely been in a mental institution recently.
Here's where the profile became one of the most accurate in early FBI history. Ressler's prediction matched Chase exactly.

On January 27, 1978, Chase entered the home of Evelyn Miroth, 38. He killed Miroth, her son Jason (6), Daniel Meredith (a family friend who was visiting), and David Ferreira (Miroth's nephew, 22 months old, whom she was babysitting).
Chase took the body of David Ferreira from the home. He then drove home and continued his consumption rituals. The disappearance of the infant added a public emergency to the case.

The break in the investigation came from a witness. A young woman who had known Chase from high school had encountered him at a shopping center on January 27, the day of the Miroth killings. She described him as disheveled, blood-stained, and incoherent. She gave investigators his name and possible address.
Sacramento detectives went to Chase's apartment on January 28, 1978. They found him at home. They found bloodstained clothing, weapons, and human tissue. They arrested him on the spot.

Inside Chase's apartment, investigators found a calendar marked with the dates of future planned attacks. The calendar referenced 44 specific dates over the coming year. Chase had effectively been planning a long campaign.
Chase was charged with six counts of first-degree murder. His trial began in 1979. The defense argued severe mental illness. The prosecution argued legal sanity at the time of the killings.
On May 8, 1979, Chase was convicted of all six murders. He was sentenced to death and transferred to San Quentin Prison's death row.
On December 26, 1980, Chase was found dead in his cell at San Quentin. He had committed suicide by overdosing on antidepressants he had hoarded over months. He was 30 years old.

The case had lasting effects on California's mental health policy. Chase's release from Beverly Manor in 1977 — over the objections of his treating psychiatrists — became a reference point in debates about psychiatric discharge protocols.

Special Agent Robert Ressler used the Chase profile as a teaching example for the rest of his career. The Behavioral Analysis Unit at Quantico studies it as one of the founding cases in modern FBI behavioral profiling.

Chase's belief system was documented in detail at trial. He believed Nazis controlled the world through tampered soap dishes. He believed his mother was poisoning him. He believed he needed fresh human and animal blood to maintain his own life. The delusions were severe and untreated.

Some researchers argue the Chase case demonstrates the consequences of underestimating discharged psychiatric patients. Others argue it demonstrates the failure of family members to maintain medication compliance after release.

The bodies of Chase's victims are buried in Sacramento area cemeteries. The Wallin family, Miroth family, and Ferreira family have spoken publicly about the case in subsequent decades, particularly in advocacy work for mental health reform.

Sacramento County Sheriff's archives and the California State Archives both hold records related to the case, including the trial transcripts, FBI behavioral analyses, and the prison file from San Quentin.

If you are researching modern American serial-killer cases or early FBI profiling, the Chase file is one of the foundational documents. Public access is available through standard archival research applications.
